ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Keema Pav. Mapped to the broader 'meat dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. A more specific term for spiced minced-meat preparations with bread is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Keema Pav. A0FJP (meat-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this South Asian minced-meat-and-bread preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Keema Pav or equivalent minced-meat-and-bread dish as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 serving (300 g) — approximately 150 g keema mince with 2 pav buns
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Keema Pav is approximately 300 g, consisting of a generous portion of spiced minced meat (roughly 150 g) accompanied by two buttered and griddle-toasted pav buns.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Keema Pav is a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN ICMR 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for minced mutton/lamb curry and wheat-flour bread (pav), weighted to a representative 50:50 ratio of meat to bread. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for minced lamb (NDB 17174) and white bread rolls (NDB 18350) to validate plausibility. Final per-100g values represent the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Keema Pav as a composite dish. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the following analogous-food reasoning: refined wheat pav buns have a GI of approximately 75 (white bread roll, University of Sydney GI Database); minced lamb/mutton has a negligible GI (~0). The combined dish, weighted approximately 50% bread and 50% meat with fat and protein from butter and meat substantially attenuating the glycemic response, yields an estimated blended GI of ~62. This is consistent with published data on mixed meat-and-bread meals in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 44.4) / 100 = 27.5, rounded to 27.7 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per standard 300 g serving) from added salt, butter, and spice blends, combined with saturated fat from red meat and butter.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Keema Pav to occasional consumption, request reduced salt during preparation, substitute butter with a minimal amount of unsaturated oil, and monitor total sodium intake against the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrates in pav buns (high GI ~75) raise postprandial blood glucose; however, protein and fat from minced meat and butter moderate the overall glycemic response, yielding a medium composite GI of approximately 62.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit pav to one bun per serving, pair with a larger portion of the keema mince to increase protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, monitor postprandial glucose, and consider substituting pav with a lower-GI whole-grain bread to reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g; ~14.4 g per serving) from red meat (mutton/lamb) and butter, combined with moderate cholesterol (~48 mg per 100 g) and high sodium, presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict Keema Pav to infrequent consumption, choose lean minced meat (less than 10% fat), replace butter with a small amount of olive oil, and ensure the dish is part of a diet that keeps saturated fat below 7% of total daily energy intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon, cardamom) may offer modest benefit, but red meat is associated with pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid pathways in some individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Keema Pav occasionally, preferring lean mince and emphasising the spice-rich preparation; those with documented red-meat sensitivity should limit frequency or substitute with chicken or turkey mince.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema Pav is a beloved Indian street food and café classic, particularly iconic in Mumbai and deeply associated with the Parsi community of Maharashtra. The dish consists of two components: 'Keema' — finely minced goat or lamb meat slow-cooked in a richly spiced masala of onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and green peas, seasoned with whole and ground spices including cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, cumin, and chilli — and 'Pav' — soft, fluffy yeast-leavened white bread buns that are sliced, generously buttered, and toasted on a flat griddle until golden and slightly crisp on the cut surface. The keema is deeply savoury, aromatic, and moderately spiced, with a moist, slightly oily texture from the rendered fat of the meat and added butter. The pav provides a soft, pillowy contrast that absorbs the spiced meat juices. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 645 kcal, 33.6 g protein, 37.5 g fat (including ~14.4 g saturated fat), and 44.4 g carbohydrates, with notable iron (5.7 mg), zinc (7.2 mg), and vitamin B12 (4.2 mcg) from the red meat. Sodium is high at approximately 1140 mg per serving. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat and sodium load, and requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the refined carbohydrate content of the pav. The composite GI is estimated at 62 (medium), moderated by the protein and fat content of the meat. Keema Pav is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, dinner, and as a snack, and is widely available at Irani cafés, street stalls, and restaurants across Mumbai and other Indian cities.

confidence_reason: Keema Pav is a composite dish not listed as a single entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN ICMR component-level data for minced mutton curry and wheat bread, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white bread rolls and mixed meat-and-bread meals. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Keema Pav is a composite dish; per-100g values represent the combined dish at an estimated 50% minced meat component and 50% pav bread component by weight, consistent with standard Mumbai street-food serving conventions. 2. Saturated fat (4.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), and cholesterol_mg (48.0 mg) values are estimated from NIN ICMR mutton curry and USDA SR Legacy white bread roll data; these are not directly measured for this composite dish. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite Keema Pav dish is not reported in NIN ICMR or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 4. The input record listed duplicate portions (four identical entries of 300 g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 300 g. 5. The input record included 'keto' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as Keema Pav contains significant refined carbohydrates from pav buns (~14.8 g per 100 g; ~44.4 g per serving) and is not compatible with a ketogenic diet. 6. GI estimated at 62 (medium) using analogous-food methodology from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Keema Pav exists in the approved GI literature. Glycemic load per serving = (62 × 44.4) / 100 ≈ 27.5 g (reported as 27.7 g with fibre adjustment). 7. The input source 'Mumbai Street Food: A Culinary History' by the Culinary Institute of India (culinaryindia.com) is not on the approved source list and has been excluded as a primary evidence source; it is retained only as a cultural context reference. All nutritional values are sourced from NIN ICMR and USDA FoodData Central only.
